text: The surgeon begins creating a flap by dissecting retrorectus medial to the semilunar line, aiming for a robust flap. The epigastric vessels are inadvertently dropped and are being swept back up into position.

The surgeon reaches the semilunar line above the arcuate line and begins a limited release of the transversus abdominis muscle to access the preperitoneal plane. The arcuate line is visible at the level of the spigelian hernia.

The surgeon observes the proximity of the Spiegelian hernia to the medial preperitoneal opening (MPO) and decides to use a single piece of mesh. A volcano sign is visible, and the surgeon begins reducing the hernia contents.

The surgeon navigates the preperitoneal space lateral to the hernia defect, carefully manipulating fat near the indirect ring while searching for cord structures to avoid injury. A lipoma is being extracted from the canal, creating space for a wide mesh overlap using a 15x15 ProGrip mesh.
The surgeon performs medial dissection in the myopectineal orifice, releasing fat from the peritoneum to access the proper plane along the cord structures.

The surgeon addresses the myopectineal orifice in a recurrent indirect hernia, identifying the vas deferens and amputating at the level of the internal ring. The cord structures and vas deferens are visible, and the surgeon begins to parietalize these structures. A lipoma is noted, potentially contributing more to the patient's symptoms than the small indirect sac.

The surgeon positions the mesh over the hernia site, ensuring it is adequately sized without using a ruler. The defect is then closed using twelve-inch zero V-lock sutures, which are challenging to handle due to the small size of the defect.
The surgeon unfolds the mesh within the preperitoneal space, ensuring proper placement over the hernia defect, and proceeds to close the flap.
